video_title: CFTC Chairman Mike Selig: Crypto Perps Are Coming to America
transcript: But we're excited to make sure that the new frontier finance is built here in the US. And that means new products, new services, new platforms, and we can't fear that. We have to embrace it and come up with a pathway for it. Because in my view, if we erect barriers, if we push this activity offshore, it will go offshore, and it's not going to benefit the American people because they're going to go use a VPN um and access it anyway, and they have none of the protections. So if we can come up with a path for the United States, we'll do it and we're going to certainly work on it. >> Bankless Nation, I am once again joined by Mike Celick. He is the CFTC chairman back on Bank list. Mike, great to have you. Glad to be here. Uh two weeks ago, the CFTC announced the order for approval to Koshi X for the listing of the BTC per contract. Uh this is a pretty pretty big landmark for the perpetual as an instrument. is also the first onshore US Bitcoin perpetual futures contract from a CFTC registered exchange. At the same time, the CFTC also gave Coinbase a no action letter allowing them to pipe their customers to Darabit uh in order to access futures options and per Coinbase access through Darab. Pretty landmark approval. Uh Mike, are we about to see more of these? Is the CFTC opening up the gate for onore? what can we expect as an industry or people who are just interested in the perpetual future instrument? >> Well, thanks for having me on the show and uh you know this is really as as you say a watershed moment, a pivotal moment where we've turned the tide on the years of regulation by enforcement, a lack of clear rules, kind of this offshoring pushing things out of the country and we want it to be here in the United States. So we want the industry to survive, to thrive, and to build here in the US. And part of that means having a futures market, having perpetual contracts that are tradable on US exchanges under US regulation on US soil and, you know, not something that you have to go use a VPN to be able to to figure out how to trade. So that starts with exchanges like KHI, exchanges like Coinbase, and there's a long list of others who will be launching the product soon. We started off uh with this initial order for a Bitcoin perpetual contract that was listed on Koshi's exchange and we also approved the ability to access through Coinbase's broker uh offshore uh perpetual contracts and all these are regulated products and that's really the theme here. This administration cares about regulation but we also care about innovation. So we are opening the doors to new products and we think this is the beginning. It's it's certainly not the end. There's many more uh crypto and other types of products that people want to offer here in the US that for so long have existed only offshore. So this is the the beginning and not the end for sure. The beginning and not the end. And I think that's why people in my neck of the woods are just so excited. PERPS as an instrument as you know Per's platforms incredibly successful as an adoption metric like just the adoption of the perp uh has gone incredibly well and Perp's platforms are just generating some of the the greatest profits out of crypto that we've seen and so this is why people are excited you know first we can start with Bitcoin but we have a whole industry of assets that we would like to propify uh maybe in the same way that we had the the ETFs you know first we started with Bitcoin and then Ether came and then there are even more ETFs. Is that something that's going to happen with uh per as well? >> Well, one important point is just in the week since we saw the greenlighting of the Bitcoin perpetual contract, we've now seen a number of products self-certified as perpetual contracts in the digital commodity world. So, we break down at the SEC and CFTC crypto assets using clear rules unlike the the last administration uh the crypto assets into categories. So we have digital commodities and these are assets that derive their value from a network or protocol or an application. Things like Bitcoin, Ethereum, Salana, uh Uniswap's token, the these types of tokens that derive value from a from a network or product or application. Uh all of those products with certain requirements around the product being listed. Uh the products have to have a ready market, have to be not readily susceptible to manipulation and so on and so forth. But provided that the contract meets our requirements, those can be self-certified. That means that the exchange can list those products without having to ask for permission first. They can essentially submit them and we can reject the contract if we believe there's an issue with our regulations. Uh but they can be listed fairly easily. So we've seen a number of those be listed in the past week. We expect to see more in the future. But then moving into other categories, digital uh you know uh collectibles for example, meme coins, those sorts of assets, we would expect a process with the staff. We want those to go through a full approval with the CFTC. And then moving into things beyond crypto, equities, which we can certainly get into metals, other types of products. We we would not be expecting those to be listed right away. There will be a process with the staff, but we're interested to engage with the with the exchanges on listing new products. So there are some crypto assets that are being given clarity by clarity and I think what I what I heard from you just now is those assets that have already kind of gone through the legislative process are able to be self-certified by CFTC approved platforms already. And this is just kind of how this goes. Uh and so if you are a CFTC compliant exchange you can self-certify given the asset itself has already been given some sort of uh approval clarity through clarity act. Did I hear that correctly? >> Well, it's not the Clarity Act itself. So, um, a couple months ago or maybe even three or four months ago now, Chairman Atkins and I came together, worked together on an interpretation that divided up crypto assets into categories. And so, we have crypto uh assets that are digital commodities, digital collectibles, digital tools, things like liquid staking receipts, um digital securities, tokenized stocks, and all of that and stable coins. And so this uh perpetual order that we granted allows for self-certification of crypto assets in the digital commodities category and then beyond that whether it's in crypto or in stocks or anything else we would expect a process with the staff but the staff's ready to engage on on other asset classes as well. >> I see. Okay. So like the big the big assets, the ones that are commonly found to be the layer 1 native assets, Bitcoin, Ether, Salana, one could one could imagine as well are all um in that category that that you just said of just like it's going to be exchanges are going to be able to self-certify. And then as we maybe get a little bit further into more of the uh newer or more exotic assets in the in the crypto industry, that is a little bit of facts and circumstances about the nature of those things. >> That's absolutely right. And we have seen I think there's about 17 other assets beyond Bitcoin that we saw self-certified by exchanges are in the process of being self-certified in the past week. >> What are these exchanges? Because there is a bit of a gold rush for the United States Pers market that players are just chomping on the bit like Coinbase, Robin Hood, Kraken, OKX, they all have United States entities that they want to you know offer per. We have the predition market platforms. Khi, which you gave you guys gave the the Bitcoin curb contract for originally, but also Poly Market was in this game as well. Uh but then we also have uh some of the more cryptonative onchain platforms uh like Hyperlid, Lighter, a few of the other ones. So many players want access to this uh the United States Pers market. How do you guys delineate between just the different nature of all these platforms? >> We're working with virtually all these platforms. So, Coinbase and Kali were the first to to launch. Uh, but we also have Bitnomial that's had a perpetual style contract for some time now. It's not a true perpetual. Uh, you can probably expect them to to move in the same direction as well as other exchanges. Um, I believe Kraken and Gemini, which Kraken Kraken actually owns Bitnomial, believe they've made announcements on social media. So, there's many others that are looking to offer the product and we're excited to work with these different exchanges. And then when you get into the onchain space, many of the considerations around onchain products are different, but we're certainly engaging with these uh protocol developers and firms to to help get onchain markets to the US as well. >> As you would expect, the per market has, you know, grown offshore and now now we're seeing it come onshore. Is the goal to get all of it onshore or how do you expect the equilibrium to kind of settle out between some offshore markets will remain offshore or uh you know chairman Mike C like is it your job as the chairman of the CFTC to like get everything onshore as much as possible? Well look I think my goal as the chairman is to get everything within a regulated market structure here in the US. So to the extent that the product exists offshore and it's something that there's interest in in the United States, we want to bring it here. We want the volume, we want the liquidity, we want that ability to engage in productive economic activity here in the United States, but we have to protect investors. We have to make sure customer funds are safe. I do not want another FTX to happen. And I will add on that point, the CFTC regulated FTX entity was the only one that was safe that that protected customer funds. And so regulation works well. This administration believes in regulation, but also in innovation. And so whether it's crypto, AI, prediction markets, new derivatives like perpetuals, we want it here in the US, but we're going to set guard rails around it. And those guardrails we believe will be best-in-class, the gold standard. We expect other countries to follow. Uh, but we are going to lead. We are going to make sure the United States is a leader in innovation. How will the pers in inside the United States work different than what is typically found offshore? Uh so like what are those guard rails that will just kind of change the nature of the perp found in the compliant US market versus what might what may be found elsewhere? One important consideration is that each of our exchanges is CFTC registered. It has to offer a rule book. It has to comply with certain core principles that are laid out in our statute. It's overseen by the CFTC. We examine each of these exchanges and then also the clearing house. So each contract is run through a central counterparty clearing house. Uh and that means that there are certain controls that protect customer funds. Oftent times you're using a broker to access the clearing house to access the exchange. You're not going direct to the market. But we are uh starting to see many exchanges offer direct access which we also think is is a valuable thing for our markets. We like competition. We like optionality. So there's a a wide range of regulations that apply here in the US to these platforms relative to say something on chain that that doesn't have a a regulator. Um we also of course approve contracts for listing. So each contract has to not be readily susceptible to manipulation. I mentioned that some of these go through a self-certification process which means that that we don't have to individually approve each one but we have the ability to reject when something's submitted for listing. So that's very important. Leverage is a big issue. So leverage offshore you see 250x sometimes you know beyond that even uh in the United States we've seen more 5 to 7x range sometimes getting up to 10. Some products like the S&P products moving outside of crypto have a higher leverage but all this is based on certain parameters that are set by our regulations and then we work with the clearing houses and with the exchanges on getting right. So for Bitcoin perpetuals and some of these products, expect to see 5 to 7, maybe getting up to 10x leverage, but nothing like you're getting offshore and and that's helpful for investors. That does protect customer funds. it may be uh you know not what people are uh excited about offshore with some of the excessive leverage but I think for the larger institutions that are looking to get into these contracts and for the average investor this is a safer product than what you see offshore uh due to that lack of you know excessive leverage and of course our um our staff are looking at these products on a regular basis they might make adjustments that that ratio there's certain calculations based on you know a trailing 30-day um average or the price and all of that that they're looking at, but rest assured that regulations apply and that's going to help keep customer funds safe. >> One of the big categories that excites me and many others in the in the crypto industry is the the rise of these onchain per platforms that are uh on blockchains and therefore are imbued with some of the things that we find exciting about blockchains. They're auditable. They're transparent to the end user. They have just inherent more more fairness. Uh and so there are some of these uh platforms that when they're on a blockchain, I would expect as a regulator are exciting to you because of their transparency, their auditability. You know, some of these things actually want to like open source their code and you know, be able to show you at the CFTC, but also me as the user exactly how the code operates. Does this change the nature of how the CFTC thinks about regulation in this space when uh some of these platforms can just you know open so open source their their guts and show the world exactly how they operate? How does that change your job if at all? Well, Bitcoin and blockchain technology really came about on the heels of the financial crisis and financial crisis in large part was this issue of a lack of transparency in our markets because they were super opaque. We now have a number of forced uh reporting and forced transparency initiatives through our regulations as a result of DoddFrank and and regulations there. But blockchain technology solves a lot of that using technology instead of kind of the the brute force of of the regulator. And so I I am very excited about that. I think that blockchain offers a ton of benefits for our traditional markets and for some of these new markets that we're seeing on the frontier. things like prediction markets, things like new crypto asset products and perpetuals. Um, I expect to to see this technology be used in a very broad uh way across our markets, whether it's in securities or commodity derivatives or the next uh new thing in our markets. So yes, I I believe that transparency can be achieved through using onchain data. The one issue that I have as a regulator is I'll give you an example. If I'm looking to catch a bad actor who's engaging in insider trading or manipulation or fraud, I can get a ton of data on chain and I likely can pinpoint that person with enough research and analytics uh to figure out who they are because they probably onboarded through one of the exchanges uh let's say they're they're trading in DeFi, but I need to to find a way to be able to catch some of these bad actors. So, you know, having uh tools, analytics tools is very helpful. I think the technology is in its early stages, but I do expect as uh onchain technology continues to advance, it will become much easier for regulators to really lean into the technology for uh us to replace things like trade reporting to to data repositories with onchain transaction data because we can see the transaction occurred uh that it settled to the Ethereum blockchain or to our layer 2 or wherever else. uh and we can figure out with a a strong degree of certainty who the traders are and and catch bad actors when, for example, they're uh trading, you know, using insider information or manipulating markets. So, I'm excited about it. I think we're in the earlier days, but we're we're getting there pretty fast. We work with uh with a number of projects on trying to to figure out how to substitute some of our existing rules and regulations for technology, how to automate things, and really lean into blockchain technology. And um of course we've got cyber risk that we're thinking about constantly, but if we can get all that right, I believe that blockchain technology really will revolutionize our financial markets in the in the near future. $100,000 is up for grabs. Now >> the the um spearhead of PERS has really been pushed forward by uh this platform Hyperlquid, the dominant in in volume and adding all these these new assets and and has really kind of just won the narrative in terms of growing the per market and it has a lot of the properties that you just talked about. you know, it was it it is on the a blockchain and has some level of transparency as a result of that, but also it is it is, you know, somewhat decentralized and therefore I I don't think Hyperlid will ever mandate or enforce KYC is just kind of antagonistic to their to their platform. So, it has some of these amazing properties, but also from the United States side of things, I don't think Hyperlid will ever do KYC. How do you think about bringing hyperlid on shore inside of the United States when it is built with this particular form factor that doesn't doesn't really fit with the pre-existing status quo? >> Yeah, saw Hunter Biden's a big fan of that one. Yeah. Um, look, I I think onchain technology, whether it's Hyperlquid or any of these onchain markets, is something that is going to, as I said, transform our markets. And and we're already seeing the the global nature of blockchain technology, uh, transform the way that that we're transacting here in the US, the way that our markets are functioning. 247 is also something that we green lit. Um, you know, we've had this in our crypto markets and predict prediction markets for some time now. Uh, but we just put out some guidance on 24/7 because exchanges are ready for it. They're excited about it. And I think that is really driven by blockchain technology, by perpetuals that are accessible 24/7, 365. uh when you're looking at these onchain uh markets, whether it's perpetuals or or other types of of derivatives markets that are onchain, there are different considerations for onchain markets. Certainly, some things are automated and smart contracts solve a lot of the things that our rules are designed to force. Um but you do have considerations. is we've seen these auto deleveraging mechanisms cause some cascades of of liquidations and really um create some adverse consequences that in the US we're not used to and I'm not going to say that they're worse for investors or better in some cases uh maybe these mechanisms work very well and they work as designed um and maybe the users trust in that code and they understand that the code will work a certain way and they're they're happy to trade in that environment but they're as a regulator later things that we have to think about and consider. So, we're very excited to see these markets flourish in the United States. We want to create a path to bring um these on markets into the United States, make sure that they comply with some form of regulation. You know, the last administration was so fixated on you have to come in and register and comply with the the rules on the books as drafted for, you know, a 1934 exchange, right? Um we're leaning into the future. We understand that our markets are changing, that technolog is changing. We want to tailor fit our rules and regulations for these new products and platforms and exchanges. Recognizing that we need to protect investors. We need to make sure that customer funds are safe or at least that there's an adequate amount of disclosure and people understand what they're trading and and how they're trading. So, we're working on all that. We've been taking a number of measures at the commission to to make sure that self-custodial wallet providers, for example, are able to offer access without having to register as a broker. We're looking at tokenization and and the use of onchain um assets for settlement of contracts and and all of that. So, we're leaning into to onchain technology. We're just getting started, but we are evaluating these platforms and engaging with many of them. We're excited to see the new frontier finance built here in the US. One thing we're observing is that real world asset pers are actually dominating volume over cryptos. Per I think it's something over over 60% of the volume of the per market uh is now looking at real world assets like oil, gold and now also these preipo stocks as well. So anthropics, basex, openai, these are all um preipo markets that you can trade with these perpetual instruments. These are American companies and so it's actually a bit weird that these markets are being hosted offshore. Do you want these markets to come offshore, these preipo uh markets to come from being offshore to onshore? It's fascinating to see all of this activity offshore. Of course, we want to see that here in the US in a regulated uh manner, but uh we're assessing it. So I I look forward to working with Chairman Atkins over at the SEC. A number of these products are security based derivatives, whether they're futures or swaps or another type of product. That's certainly something that we will work through together. Um, but but we're excited to make sure that the new frontier finance is built here in the US. And that means new products, new services, new platforms, and we can't fear that. We have to embrace it and come up with a pathway for it. Because in my view, if we erect barriers, if we push this activity offshore, it will go offshore and it's not going to benefit the American people because they're going to go use a VPN um and access it anyway and they have none of the protections. So, if we can come up with a path for the United States, we'll do it and we're going to certainly work on it. These equity per instruments that are people are getting excited about is kind of interesting because it's a perp so therefore it's um under the purview of the CFTC, but it's an equity. So it's under the purview of the SEC. It's like a perfectly hybrid instrument that requires both of your guys' collaboration, but therefore, you know, project crypto and the harmonization of the CFTC and SEC. So I'm not really worried about that. But is this unprecedented or are there other contracts, derivative contracts that are around equity instruments that have required CFTC or and SEC collaboration in the past like outside of crypto or is this something that's relatively uh relatively new for these two agencies? >> Well, security futures, so whether it's a perpetual or not, those products require joint uh authorization by both agencies. So the CFTC regulates the platforms, the exchanges that offer these products and then the exchange then has to notice register. So it goes through a a a shortened abbreviated registration process with the SEC and approval process there. So we have collaborated on that in the past, but unfortunately the CFTC and SEC have always been in these turf battles fighting with one another and so they've not wanted to work together to bring these products to market. And so usually um the products sort of wind up on the sidelines and and don't make it to market. We're working together. We want to get this right. We want to bring security futures, security perpetuals, and other types of assets to market. Uh we are working together to make sure also that persons trying to offer these products and engage in these markets aren't caught in the crosshairs like the Gary Gendler, Russ Benham years where the two agencies were just bringing enforcement actions right and left. you know, you go and comply with the CFTC and then the SEC brings an enforcement action, you comply with the CFTC, uh, you know, and the the the opposite. So, um, we're working to fix all of that and, um, and we're optimistic that we're going to see these products in the near future. One thing I might think could be coming down the pike for this perp instrument, this this fight over the perp uh is that the the crypto offshore platforms, the the the startups, the more nimble moving companies have been really first to move here. But a perp is just an instrument. It is a neutral instrument can be applied anywhere. And I would actually expect some of the incumbents like the CME group and the the the commodities gargantuans that are already out there are also looking at this uh and you know they they own the commodities volume they own like the the wheat and oil and you know uh all all that kind of stuff. How as a regulator do you balance the the disruptors, you know, the Coinbases, the Robin Hoods, the hyperlquids, because you want these markets to come on shore and be compliant, but we have these gargantuan com incumbents with all the commodities who are just going to be slow to move, but they they do all of the actual like delivery of the actual real commodity underlying, which is like a nuance that I don't think Hyperlid wants to at all deal with when it trades oil. And so there's going to be a little bit of like Tetrising behind who gets what. Uh, and I don't really know how that unfolds, but as a regulator, you're going to be at the center of this. I would imagine this is me kind of just like future escaping here. Uh, is is what I'm talking about real. Is that is there a real fight down the line between these incumbents and the disruptors over like the turf war of the per? Well, look, I I I think with all of these uh various products, we want to bring them into the US in a regulated context. I I think the the two agencies certainly have to work together to get all this right. Um when it comes to incumbent exchanges, our mandate as an agency is fair competition. So, we're not standing in the way of any incumbents from coming into these markets and offering new products. We love to see innovation. We love to see uh competition between new entrance and old entrance. So, so we're here for that. Uh certainly with some of these exchanges, they're dy registered and there's aspects where the two agencies, the CFTC and SEC will need to work together to make sure they can bring their products to market. But we're excited about that. Um what I do want to get clear though is that when it comes to um some of the uh allegations around what products are available for trading, we've been very clear. we are starting and and the president also asked for this in his president's working group report. This is something that's very much in line with what the administration has already said uh many months ago. We're coming to to the market with crypto per uh we will consider other products whether it's agricultural. We we are concerned about that. We don't necessarily think that people uh that there's a place for corn perpetual contracts. We actually haven't gotten requests for that. But to the extent uh firms want to engage on that will consider it. But to your point, the deliverable supply issue, looking at the commercial realities, some of these contracts, they're dated for a reason because the commodity comes to harvest during that period. Uh there's other seasonal changes in the market. And so it may not make sense to have a corn or a live cattle perpetual contract. And we certainly have not uh green lit any of that. We've been very clear in our release that that's something that we would expect heavy engagement with the staff if someone were to try to bring to market. There are other products that uh may be closer to crypto that that we're um happy to engage on. But the two areas where we expect engagement the near term are additional crypto products and some of these equity products and and that equity engagement will be between the the SEC and the CFTC. Maybe I'm getting a little a bit ahead of myself then in the sense that we have a whole entire crypto industry that we need to bring onshore using the per instrument. We will also talk about the equity pers because that is simply just relevant that's here today. Uh, and as the PERP grows and the CME group and the incumbents inside of the in Chicago, they'll get to they'll do their PERP things in the future, but let's just worry about that then in 2027 2028 uh down the line and we'll just worry about what's happening here and now. That's that's what I just heard from you. >> That's right. And look, some of these incumbents may not like per offer per too. And that's totally fine. And I think there could be competition between a 24/7 model that someone wants to offer and feels comfortable with versus a perpetual 24/7 model. So I think there will be all sorts of options uh available to to the market and the marketplace will decide. I think it's a you know free uh economy and people can go purchase the assets they want to purchase. Uh but we're going to make sure that there's rules and regulations in place to protect investors when they do. So on the the crypto side of things, uh crypto volume has really flipped to be about 90% per volume, 10% spot. So it's gotten pretty topheavy. Are we concerned about the deriv derivative markets being so topheavy and potentially causing issues potentially systemic if just the volumes are all derivative based and spot actually kind of thins out? Well, if you look at other commodity asset classes, this is not uncommon because you have so much leverage in the derivatives markets. So our global derivatives markets are actually 1.2 quadrillion notional. So there's a lot of trading volume there. Uh if you think about that as well. Uh it's it's just a lot more to be traded. So that's correct. But but I don't necessarily think it it tells the whole story. I think this is something if you mapped on other commodities to that same framework, you'd have a similar amount of leverage in the system, a similar amount of volume disparity from spot to uh the futures market. and and also in particular with commodities, the futures market's much more liquid and and you know, you've got the ability to go trade on an exchange, whereas with a with live cattle, you're going to an auction. So, it's a little bit different markettomarket, but it's certainly something that we're looking at and thinking about. Uh the the core concern for us is that these markets are not readily susceptible to manipulation. So if we're looking at, for example, Bitcoin relative to, you know, some meme coin of choice, Pepe or something like that, you know, we're going to think about how much uh supply there is, how much spot trading, how much uh susceptibility there is to manipulation in the spot relative to the futures. >> Chairman, it's been fantastic having you on once again. Uh I also just appreciate it as a content producer being able to access people like you and get answers directly from people like you. Gary Gendler never came on the show despite our invitation. So I appreciate you. The crypto industry is pretty bullish persp. Uh how do you think about such a bullish notion like that? Maybe maybe we need to tamper our expectations. Or maybe you also think that all of these platforms also might just add per uh maybe a little bit more modest uh with leverage than we see on the crypto side of things, but nonetheless, everything will get perpified. How do you think about this question? >> The CFTC hasn't approved a new type of derivative instrument for over a decade. I I think it's incredibly exciting that we now have perpetuals in the lineup and we're going to continue to engage with the exchanges on bringing some more of these products to market. So, this is just the beginning. As I said at the outset, it's not the end. We expect to see more of these products and we'll continue to monitor the uh market and and make sure that investors are protected, but we're also seeing innovation in our markets. And uh I'm very excited to have conversations with the SEC about equity perpetuals and other types of products. So I I think it's a it's a time to be excited.
